questions.question: On macOS and Linux, what is the primary method to install Claude Code in one go?
options: Using the curl command
Using Homebrew with brew install
Using the winget command
Using the Invoke-RestMethod command
questions.explanation: The curl command is the primary method for installing Claude Code on macOS, Linux, and WSL in one go. While Homebrew is mentioned as an alternative, it does not support auto-updates. The other options are specific to Windows environments.

questions.question: After installing Claude Code in the terminal, what command do you run to start it?
options: claude-start
claude
claude-init
claude-run
questions.explanation: The command to start Claude Code in the terminal is simply `claude`. This command is run from your project directory and gives Claude Code access to that directory and all of its subfolders.

questions.question: In VS Code, how do you open Claude Code after installation?
options: Run the `claude` command in the integrated terminal
Use the command palette with Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P and search for 'Claude Code Open in New Tab'
Restart VS Code and wait for automatic activation
Install a separate VS Code extension pack
questions.explanation: After installing the Claude Code extension in VS Code, you open it by using the command palette (Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P) and searching for 'Claude Code Open in New Tab.' You can also click the Claude logo in the sidebar if it's visible. The other options are either incorrect or incomplete.

questions.question: What is a key limitation of using Homebrew or winget to install Claude Code?
options: They only work on Windows systems
They do not support auto-updates
They require manual authentication each time
They cannot access project subdirectories
questions.explanation: Both Homebrew (on macOS/Linux) and winget (on Windows) do not support auto-updates for Claude Code. This is why the curl command is recommended as the primary installation method, as it enables automatic updates. The other options are not supported by the source material.

questions.question: On Windows, which of the following are valid methods to install Claude Code in the terminal?
options: PowerShell with Invoke-RestMethod and CMD with curl only
CMD with curl and winget only
Invoke-RestMethod in PowerShell, curl in CMD, and winget
Homebrew and Invoke-RestMethod only
questions.explanation: On Windows, there are three installation options: Invoke-RestMethod in PowerShell, curl in CMD, and winget. All three are valid methods, though winget (like Homebrew) does not support auto-updates. Homebrew is not available on Windows.

questions.question: During initial setup in the terminal, what authentication options are available for Claude Code?
options: Only Claude account subscription (Pro or Max)
Claude account subscription, API key, or third-party platform
Only API key authentication
GitHub authentication only
questions.explanation: During initial setup, users can choose between signing in with a Claude account (Pro, Max, or Enterprise) or using an API key. Third-party platform authentication is also available. Enterprise users should select the Claude Enterprise account option if their organization has one.

questions.question: What is the scope of file access when you run Claude Code from a specific directory?
options: Only files in that directory, excluding subdirectories
That directory and all of its subfolders
All files on the system
Only files with specific extensions
questions.explanation: When you run Claude Code in a directory, it has access to that directory and all of its subfolders. This means you can work with the entire project structure from the point where you launch the command.

questions.question: In the JetBrains IDE, how do you access Claude Code after installation?
options: Click the Claude logo to open a pane with the terminal experience
Use the command palette to search for Claude Code
Install a separate marketplace plugin
Run a terminal command within the IDE
questions.explanation: After installing the Claude Code plugin from the JetBrains Marketplace and restarting the IDE, you click the Claude logo to open a pane that provides the terminal experience. This pane works alongside your editor, allowing you to use Claude Code without leaving your IDE.

questions.question: What distinguishes the Claude Desktop Code experience from the web version?
options: Desktop supports cloud environments while web does not
Web version supports all repositories while desktop is limited to GitHub
Desktop allows working in specific folders and changing permissions, while web is restricted to GitHub repositories
Desktop requires internet connection while web works offline
questions.explanation: The Claude Desktop Code experience allows you to work in a specific folder, change permissions, and even work in cloud environments. In contrast, the web version (accessed at claude.ai/code) is restricted to working with GitHub repositories. Both require internet connectivity.

questions.question: If you want to ensure you have access to the latest Claude Code features as soon as they are released, which installation method should you prioritize?
options: JetBrains IDE integration for the most stable experience
Web version for cross-platform compatibility
Terminal installation for cutting-edge feature access
Desktop application for background operation
questions.explanation: The terminal is the best choice for staying on the cutting edge because features ship there first. While IDE integrations and desktop provide nearly identical experiences, they may lag behind the terminal in feature releases. The web version is more limited in scope.

questions.question: You need to work on a project remotely using GitHub repositories while maintaining a lightweight setup. Which Claude Code installation method best fits this scenario?
options: Terminal installation with SSH access to remote servers
JetBrains IDE integration for remote development
Web version at claude.ai/code for GitHub repository access
Desktop application with cloud environment support
questions.explanation: The web version at claude.ai/code is specifically designed for remote work on projects through GitHub repositories. While the desktop supports cloud environments, the web version is the most straightforward solution for GitHub-based remote work. Terminal and IDE integrations are better suited for local development.

questions.question: An organization with a Claude Enterprise account wants to ensure all developers use the correct authentication method during terminal setup. What should they instruct their team to do?
options: Use API key authentication for consistency across all developers
Select the Claude Enterprise account option during initial setup
Use individual Pro or Max subscriptions for flexibility
Avoid terminal installation and use only IDE integrations
questions.explanation: Organizations with a Claude Enterprise account should instruct their team to select the Claude Enterprise account option during initial setup. This ensures proper authentication and access control at the organizational level. While API keys are an option, using the Enterprise account option is the recommended approach for enterprise deployments.
